summary: P.J. Karafiol describes Math Circles of Chicago, a free math enrichment program he founded in 2010 to address elitism in math education. The program now serves 500 students across five locations in Chicago, including some of the city's poorest neighborhoods, focusing on collaborative problem-solving rather than lectures or competitions. The organization operates on a donor-supported 'NPR model' with no financial barriers to entry—just a handwritten essay about why students want to participate. Karafiol argues that for many students, math circle provides their only exposure to 'real mathematics' as opposed to arithmetic and rote procedures taught in schools.
key_points: Math Circles of Chicago is 100% free and donor-supported, serving 500 students in grades 5-12 across five locations including Chicago's poorest neighborhoods
The program focuses on collaborative problem-solving (graph theory, number theory, geometry) rather than lectures, competitions, or worksheets
The only admission requirement is a handwritten essay about why students want to do math—no academic prerequisites or fees
The organization sponsors Chicago's only youth math research symposium (QED) where 150+ students present original research
Karafiol distinguishes between 'arithmetic' taught in schools and 'real mathematics,' arguing most students never encounter the latter
money_quotes: Mathematics was what was in those books; the subject I hated in school was arithmetic.
For many of the kids we serve, math circle is the only exposure they get to what I (or my father) would call real mathematics.
Students have to write, by hand (no typing allowed!), a one-page essay about why they want to do math on Saturdays.
We've shown that math circles can exist (and thrive) outside of traditional university environments.
Our programs are currently growing faster than our fundraising–which is a great problem to have.
key_insight: Making math enrichment accessible requires removing financial barriers entirely and meeting students in their own communities, not expecting them to come to elite institutions.
